Why rubber

Built for Nigerian
market realities

Most crops suffer when the naira falls. Rubber does the opposite. Here is why it is one of the most strategically sound agricultural investments in Nigeria today.

N4,500

per kg for processed TSR rubber — peak Nigerian market price

💱

Dollar-linked income

Natural rubber is priced in USD globally. Every time the naira weakens against the dollar, your income in naira goes up — automatically. Devaluation is your ally, not your enemy.

📅

28 years from a single planting

Plant once. Tap three times a week for 28 years. The tree works for you across four panels in a complete rotational cycle — the original long-term income machine.

🌍

Global demand, guaranteed buyers

Bridgestone, Michelin, Continental, Goodyear — the world's biggest tyre manufacturers buy natural rubber continuously. China alone accounts for 40% of global demand. Your harvest has a buyer before it drips.

🏡

Perfect for South-West Nigeria

Ogun and Oyo States have the ideal combination of deep soils (1.5m+), correct pH (4.5–6.0), rainfall (1,200–2,000mm/year), and existing rubber farming infrastructure through RRIN and the ADPs.

"White gold drips from every cut. The tree produces for 28 years. The farmer collects three times a week."

Hevea brasiliensis · Latex Collection
Certified clones

Choose the right
clone

The clone you plant determines your yield for 28 years. Never plant uncertified seedlings. These are the three recommended varieties for Nigerian conditions.

⭐ Top recommendation

RRIM 600

The gold standard for Nigerian rubber farms. Wind-resistant, high-yielding, disease-tolerant. The most widely planted certified clone in West Africa.

OriginMalaysia · RRIM
Yield1,500–2,000 kg/ha
Wind resistanceExcellent
Panel life7 years per panel
High yield

PB 235

High-yielding Malaysian clone. Excellent latex production per cut. Widely available through RRIN and state ADPs across South-West Nigeria.

OriginMalaysia · Prang Besar
Yield1,400–1,900 kg/ha
Bark renewalGood
AvailabilityRRIN / ADP
Drought tolerant

IRCA 18

Ivory Coast-developed clone with strong drought tolerance. Well-suited to areas of Oyo State with lower or more variable rainfall patterns.

OriginIRCA · Côte d'Ivoire
Yield1,200–1,700 kg/ha
Drought toleranceHigh
Best forDrier zones
The tapping system

How to tap
correctly

Tapping is both art and science. The wrong depth, the wrong time, or the wrong tool causes permanent bark damage that reduces yield for the entire remaining life of the tree.

01

Readiness check

Never tap before the tree is ready. The girth must be ≥50cm at 100cm from ground level. This typically occurs at Year 5–7. Tapping a tree too early permanently stunts its productive capacity.

Girth ≥50cm at 1m height
02

Opening cut

Use a metal ribbon to mark a 30° angle down-sloping cut. Mark with an awl first. Deepen with the tapping gouge to exactly 4.5mm depth — reaching the lactiferous vessels in the deepest bark layer without ever touching the cambium.

4.5mm depth — never deeper
03

Timing is everything

Tap between 5:00 AM and 6:30 AM only. Turgor pressure (internal tree pressure that drives latex flow) is highest at dawn. Tapping in the heat of the day produces a fraction of the latex yield. Each tapper can manage 440 trees per day at this pace.

5:00 AM – 6:30 AM only
04

Daily cut

Each tapping removes exactly 1.5mm of bark downward. Stop 1.5mm from the cambium — the living layer between bark and wood. Damage the cambium and that section of the panel is permanently dead. Sharpness of your gouge is non-negotiable.

1.5mm per cut · stop 1.5mm from cambium
05

Collection

Return after 3–4 hours to collect latex from cups. Add 0.5% ammonia solution to fresh latex immediately to prevent coagulation during transport. Rinse cups daily. Record yield per tree monthly to identify under-producing trees.

Collect after 3–4 hours
06

The 28-year panel cycle

Start at 1.5m height on the first panel. Work downward over 7 years. Switch to the opposite side for 7 years. Return to the first side (bark has regenerated). Complete 3 full cycles over 28 years. Each panel gives 7 years of bark. Never rush a panel.

4 panels · 7 years each · 28-year cycle

"From a 4am start to a market in Benin City, Singapore, or Akron, Ohio — the journey of Nigerian rubber spans continents."

Coagulated Rubber · Farm Gate · Ogun State
Tapping inscriptions decoded

What the marks on
your trees mean

Every commercial rubber tree carries a coded record of its history, system, and status. Most Nigerian farmers cannot read these marks. This manual decodes every one of them.

W

White inscriptions

Active tree information — painted in white

#247

Tree number

Unique identifier per tree. Used for yield tracking and disease records.

RRIM

Clone code

Identifies the certified clone variety. Critical for predicting tapping behaviour.

S/2 d/3

Tapping system

Half-spiral (S/2) tapped every 3rd day (d/3). The RECOMMENDED system balancing yield and tree health.

HO

Panel designation

High Opening panel. LO = Low Opening. Tracks which panel is active and from which side.

ET

Ethephon stimulant ring

Marks trees receiving chemical yield stimulation. Requires careful application — overuse causes brown bast.

B

Black inscriptions

Warning marks — painted in black or red

X / D

Diseased — DO NOT TAP

Tree has confirmed disease. Tapping spreads infection to your tools and neighbouring trees. Stop immediately.

R

Resting panel

This panel is in its recovery period. Bark is regenerating. Tapping a resting panel wastes the regeneration and shortens panel life.

W

Wounded

Panel has been accidentally cut beyond the safe depth. Allow healing before resuming tapping on this section.

E

Experimental

Tree is under a trial — stimulant test, spacing study, or variety research. Record data; do not alter the management.

——

Horizontal stop line

The most critical mark on any tree. NEVER cut below this line. It marks the maximum safe tapping depth before permanent damage.

25-year financial projection

5 hectares of rubber —
year by year

Based on RRIM 600 clone, 476 trees/ha (3m × 7m spacing), Ogun State conditions. Nigerian market prices — N600–N4,500/kg depending on processing level.

Year Phase Yield/ha (kg) Revenue (5 ha) Progress
1–4Immature—Intercrop income
5–6Early bearing300–600N2.4M
7Building900N3.8M
8Growing1,100N4.7M
10Peak approach1,500N6.8M
12–20⭐ Peak production2,000N9.5M/year
21–28Mature1,500–1,800N6M–N7.6M

Nigerian market prices

What your rubber
is worth

The more you process, the more you earn. Fresh latex at farm gate is the entry point. Technically Specified Rubber (TSR) commands global commodity prices.

Farm gate · unprocessed
Fresh Field Latex
N600–N900
per kg
Sold directly to dealers or local processors. Minimum effort, minimum price. Best for small farms without processing equipment.
Coagulated · dried
Cup Lump / Scrap
N800–N1,200
per kg
Coagulated latex collected from cups. Dried and sold in slabs. Simple processing, immediate step up in value per kg.
Processed · grade
ADS / RSS Grade
N2,000–N3,500
per kg
Air-Dried Sheet or Ribbed Smoked Sheet. Processed, graded rubber commanding significantly higher prices from factories.
Export grade · global
TSR 20 / RSS 1
N2,800–N4,500
per kg
Technically Specified Rubber. Export-grade product priced against global commodity markets. Buyers: Bridgestone, Continental, Michelin.
